verse_specific_anchors: the small-well-vs-flood structural analogy (yāvān artha udapāne... sarvataḥ samplutodake) maps directly onto rule (small artifact) vs. principle (flood) — this is the verse's own structure, not a generic engineering platitude

the realized-only scoping (brāhmaṇasya vijānataḥ) maps onto 'the practitioner who has reached the principle' — the falsifiability and counter-example clauses both turn on this scoping, which would not survive being attached to a different verse

the symmetric two-pathology framing (rule-hostage past mastery + premature-mastery before apprentice work) is a reading specific to this verse's well/flood structure — premature-mastery is the inverse of 'announcing flood while standing at the well'
verdict_reason: The analog is anchored in the verse's specific small-well/flood structural analogy and in the realized/unrealized scoping (vijānataḥ). Imagine attaching this analog to other Ch. 2 verses: BG 2.47 (kuru karmāṇi — your right is to action, not fruit) does not concern rule-internalization at all; BG 2.48 (samatvam yoga ucyate) is about operator equanimity, not rule-fluency; BG 2.55-72 (sthita-prajña) describes the steady operator's character broadly, not the well-vs-flood dynamic specifically. The analog could not be relocated to those verses without losing its core anchor — the well/flood asymmetry. The 'rules dissolve into mastery' move is uniquely licensed by this verse's literal structure. Pass.
